coredata.dc:title: Cutting-edge tumor nanotherapy: Advancements in 5-fluorouracil Drug-loaded chitosan nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Hossein Karami, Mohammad
Abdouss, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Currently, cancer continues to be a significant contributor to global mortality rates. Scientists and researchers are actively working to find efficient approaches for cancer treatment that minimize potential negative consequences. As a result, nanoparticles (NPs) have emerged as potential vehicles for carrying anti-inflammatory drugs and improving the transportation of drugs during chemotherapy. Chitosan nanoparticles (CSNPs) have attracted considerable interest among various NPs. This is primarily due to their cost-effectiveness, compatibility with biological systems, ability to degrade naturally, and non-toxic characteristics. CSNPs are an ideal option for drug delivery due to their unique attributes. The pH-responsive nature of chitosan allows for precise drug dispersion within the cancerous microenvironment, making it a highly promising carrier system. Although various anti-cancer drugs are utilized in chemotherapy, it remains the most prevalent form of treatment. A commonly prescribed medication for the treatment of solid cancers is 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU). It can be administered either as a standalone treatment or in conjunction with other chemotherapy regimens. However, the efficacy of 5-FU as a chemotherapeutic agent is impeded by its inconsistent absorption by the body, short duration of action, and the necessity for frequent dosing, which leads to unfavorable effects. This review provides researchers with the most recent and comprehensive insights into the progress made in developing nanocarriers for delivering 5-FU. The review specifically emphasizes the importance of nanocarrier size and highlights their potential as highly effective tools for cancer treatment.

row.entry: Formulated nanoparticles in an in-vitro study on colorectal cancer showed controlled drug release and no associated toxicity.

The nano-hybrid exhibits a localized response to changes in pH, allowing for precise drug release at specific sites in the body. Additionally, it effectively reduces potential toxicity, and boasts an impressive drug loading capacity of 90 %. As a result, it enhances both the accuracy of diagnoses and the effectiveness of therapeutic interventions.

In the field of skin cancer research, a study was conducted on live BALB/c mice to examine the effectiveness of a combination treatment. The results demonstrated that this treatment significantly reduced the required doses of 5-FU by a magnitude of 6.66 times. Furthermore, it also led to a remarkable reduction of 82.39 % in the average disease burden and tumor volume.

The application of chitosan modified liposomes displayed exceptional efficacy in capturing drug molecules, providing an extended duration of drug release, and significantly enhancing the ability to kill HT-29 cancer cells. These remarkable benefits were achieved through the incorporation of a chitosan coating, which ensured a sustained and controlled delivery system.
Improve the duration of circulation in the body, achieve targeted accumulation specifically in tumor cells, and exhibit enhanced toxicity against them.

The drug-incorporated nanomaterials displayed a two-phase spread and exhibited enhanced effectiveness against cancer. Among these, the C-5-FUPLGANPs showcased the highest level of toxicity, resulting in a significant 66 % reduction in the viability of tumor cells.
Improved medication loading, targeted release, superior anticancer activity.

The application of NPs demonstrated exceptional cytotoxicity, significantly reducing cell viability to 24.14. Moreover, the NPs exhibited a drug release mechanism that was dependent on the acidity levels found in the colon.
Chemotherapy and hyperthermia induced by AuNCs collaborate synergistically to exhibit enhanced effectiveness in combating cancer, specifically targeting the malignant HT29 cell line.
It has been demonstrated that this compound exhibits exceptional stability and remarkable effectiveness against tumor growth, illustrated by its IC₅₀ value that is as low as 66.53 μM.
Highly efficient substance capture (95 % for quercetin, 75 % for 5-FU) and significant reduction of malignant cells (70 %).
The stimuli-sensitive polymer coating displays a postponed release of medication when exposed to a mildly alkaline pH of 6.8. It shows exceptional stability and improved cellular uptake capability.
This drug has been proven to possess excellent biocompatibility, its release is dependent on pH levels, and it effectively reduces any potential negative effects.
Concurrent treatment enhances ALA therapy efficacy..
Nanoparticles enhance anti-cancer properties, manage ROS generation, reduce toxicity.
Nano carriers possess stability, induce cell death, despite non-specific uptake.
Aptamer-modified nanoparticles enhance cell intake, increase toxicity, reduce IC50 value specifically in MUC1-positive cancer cells.
Improve the selectivity and targeting of drugs within tumor cells by attaching them to folate. Reduce the harmful effects on normal cells by minimizing their toxicity.
It demonstrated extraordinary capabilities in inhibiting cancer growth in MCF-7 cells while offering precisely targeted delivery and minimizing adverse immune responses.
Combinatorial treatment decreased the removal of nano-encapsulated 5-FU and curcumin within a time frame of 72 h, while also diminishing the harmful effects of 5-FU on colon cancer cells.

row.entry: Emerging potential of 5-Fluorouracil-loaded chitosan nanoparticles in cancer therapy
5-FU loaded chitosan nano-formulations in the treatment of various cancers, Employment of chitosan nanocarriers in combinatorial therapy for cancer

Cancer and its complications present a significant issue for global health. One major difficulty in treating cancer is delivering chemotherapy specifically to target cancer cells while minimizing damage to healthy cells. One method being explored is using chitosan-based nanoparticles to transport drugs and natural products. These nanoparticles enhance the efficiency and precision of chemotherapy, while also improving availability and decreasing toxicity. Research has looked into the anti-cancer effects of CSNPs, as well as their combination with the chemotherapy drug 5-FU. However, further clinical evidence is necessary to assess the efficacy and safety of CSNPs.
Folic acid decorated chitosan nanoparticles and its derivatives for the delivery of drugs and genes to cancer cells
Folic acid conjugated chitosan for the delivery of drugs, Overview of cancer drug delivery using folic acid–chitosan conjugation

Intelligent carriers made of folic acid-chitosan nanoparticles have the potential for delivering antitumor agents specifically to tumors. The co-delivery of drugs and genes to cancer cells using folate receptors has been found to be highly effective with low toxicity. Nanotheranostics are systems that merge targeted therapy with nanotechnology, aiming to enhance the effectiveness and safety of cancer treatments. Additional research and improvements are necessary to advance folate chitosan nanoparticles as a cutting-edge drug delivery system for reducing cancer recurrence and personalized chemotherapy.

Recent Advances in Designing 5-Fluorouracil
Delivery Systems: A Stepping Stone in the SafeTreatment of Colorectal Cancer
Potential Strategies to Enhance Chemotherapy by 5-FU, Combinational Delivery System Containing 5-FU

The use of advanced drug delivery systems (DDSs) in the pharmaceutical field has revolutionized the medical system by reducing side effects and increasing the efficacy of drugs. In particular, nano-based delivery systems for 5-FU have shown potential to improve cancer treatment by targeting cancer-affected areas. Nanomedicine is leading the way in personalized medicine. Various nanocarriers such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), liposomes, polymeric nanoparticles (NPs), silica NPs, and dendrimer micelles have advantages over traditional administration methods. This new DDS strategy can also improve the effectiveness of colorectal cancer treatment and overcome drug resistance. Stimuli-responsive nanocarriers allow for precise control of 5-FU release and minimize severe side effects. Future advancements in nanomedicine-based DDSs offer promise for curing cancer, but more research is needed to fully understand their potential.

Chitosan-Based Nanoparticles with Optimized Parameters for
Targeted Delivery of a Specific Anticancer Drug-AComprehensive Review
Examples of CS-based nanoparticles for effective targeted delivery of antimetabolites,Encapsulation of Multicomponent Plant Extracts

In conclusion, current literature reports suggest that unmodified chitosan nanoparticles are not recommended as anticancer drug carriers due to inadequate target delivery and stability issues. However, the utilization of modified chitosan nanoparticles with pH- and redox-sensitivity and targeting ligands such as folic acid may enhance drug efficacy and decrease side effects. Additionally, combining naturally derived compounds with cytostatic agents shows promise in overcoming drug resistance in cancer treatment. Further research is needed to optimize carrier parameters and assess the effectiveness of chitosan nanoparticles in delivering various anticancer drugs. Overall, this approach offers a potentially effective and feasible alternative to traditional drug administration.

head.ce:title: Cutting-edge tumor nanotherapy: Advancements in 5-fluorouracil Drug-loaded chitosan nanoparticles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, cancer continues to be a significant contributor to global mortality rates. Scientists and researchers are actively working to find efficient approaches for cancer treatment that minimize potential negative consequences. As a result, nanoparticles (NPs) have emerged as potential vehicles for carrying anti-inflammatory drugs and improving the transportation of drugs during chemotherapy. Chitosan nanoparticles (CSNPs) have attracted considerable interest among various NPs. This is primarily due to their cost-effectiveness, compatibility with biological systems, ability to degrade naturally, and non-toxic characteristics. CSNPs are an ideal option for drug delivery due to their unique attributes. The pH-responsive nature of chitosan allows for precise drug dispersion within the cancerous microenvironment, making it a highly promising carrier system. Although various anti-cancer drugs are utilized in chemotherapy, it remains the most prevalent form of treatment. A commonly prescribed medication for the treatment of solid cancers is 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU). It can be administered either as a standalone treatment or in conjunction with other chemotherapy regimens. However, the efficacy of 5-FU as a chemotherapeutic agent is impeded by its inconsistent absorption by the body, short duration of action, and the necessity for frequent dosing, which leads to unfavorable effects. This review provides researchers with the most recent and comprehensive insights into the progress made in developing nanocarriers for delivering 5-FU. The review specifically emphasizes the importance of nanocarrier size and highlights their potential as highly effective tools for cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Several approaches have been developed for the detection and management of cancer, which can be categorized into three main categories: immunotherapy, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy. Immunotherapy works by boosting the patient’s immune system to recognize and destroy cancer cells. In contrast, radiotherapy uses ionizing radiation to eliminate tumors or cancer cells [1].

In contrast, chemotherapy relies on the use of cytotoxic drugs to fight tumors and cancers [2–4]. The presence of fluorine in 5-FU has been found to inhibit tumor growth and shows potential for developing new medications. Combination therapy involving fluorine has been seen to enhance the cytotoxic effects of chemotherapy [5–7].

Nanomedicine, a branch of nanotechnology, focuses on using materials at the nanoscale for the diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment of diseases, including cancer. Scientists have synthesized nanoparticles and studied their toxicity on cancer cell lines, showing the potential of nanoscale materials in cancer research and treatment [9–12].

Research has specifically examined nickel oxide nanoparticles and their impact on HepG2 cells [13]. There is a growing interest in developing precise drug delivery using nanoparticles [14–16]. Targeted drug delivery through nanoparticles allows for more efficient treatment of tumor cells with lower doses. Recent studies have successfully synthesized nanoparticles loaded with drugs that specifically target tumor sites, resulting in improved efficacy [17].

For example, gelatin nanoparticles loaded with Concanavalin-A showed toxic effects on lung cancer cell lines. Furthermore, iron oxide nanoparticles loaded with 5-Fu and Pt were synthesized, resulting in apoptosis induction in 4T1 breast cancer cell lines [18].

Fig. 1 shows the various types of nanoparticles utilized for drug delivery, which are designed to transport drugs to the surface, core, or both. In recent years, there has been a substantial amount of research focused on creating drug formulations using nanotechnology [19–22].

Nanoparticles enable precise drug delivery to cancer cells, enhancing treatment effectiveness and decreasing side effects. Recent studies have demonstrated successful utilization of drug-loaded nanoparticles in the treatment of esophageal and breast cancer, highlighting the potential of nanoparticle-based therapies. However, only a small percentage of these nanosystems have undergone market evaluation, with a limited number receiving final approval for clinical use [23].
Doxil, Oncaspar, DaunoXome, and Onivyde are examples of FDA-approved nanoparticle-based drugs used in the treatment of various types of cancer [24–26].

Various chitosan-based nanoformulations have been studied for delivering 5-FU in cancer treatment. The best approach may vary depending on factors such as drug loading, controlled release, biocompatibility, target specificity, and safety [27]. Some chitosan-based nanoformulations for delivering 5-FU include chitosan nanoparticles, chitosan-coated liposomes, chitosan micelles, and chitosan-based hydrogels. Each type has its own advantages and limitations. For example, chitosan nanoparticles are known for their high drug loading and release abilities, while chitosan-coated liposomes provide improved stability and biocompatibility [28]. Chitosan micelles enhance solubility and allow for targeted delivery to cancer cells, while chitosan-based hydrogels can sustain release and localize distribution of 5-FU. Choosing the most suitable formulation requires considering specific needs for cancer treatment, like purpose, release profile, and interactions with biological substances. In vitro and in vivo studies are crucial for assessing the effectiveness and safety of nanoformulations that deliver 5-FU in cancer therapy [29]. Ultimately, finding the best chitosan-based nanoformulation for delivering 5-FU in cancer treatment will involve a thorough evaluation of these factors and the supporting research.

Le et al. fabricated PEGylated nanoliposomes with folic acid conjugation, resulting in highly effective nanodelivery systems for cancer treatment. These liposomes loaded with 5-Fu and modified with folate-PEG exhibited enhanced toxicity towards cancer cells, mediated by folate-mediated endocytosis, compared to unmodified and PEG-modified liposomes [30]. Pourmadadi et al. created a chitosan/agarose nanoemulsion with 5-FU/CUR for treating melanoma and skin conditions. The formulation improved drug retention, penetration, and showed therapeutic potential. Smith et al. created a solid lipid nanoparticle (SLN) system, 5-FU-SLN4, for improved 5-FU delivery in colorectal cancer [31]. The formulation showed potent cytotoxicity, enhanced pharmacokinetics, and reduced tumor formation in mice. Haggag et al. developed a novel method for creating 5-FU-loaded NPs using a modified double emulsion method. The unique nanocarrier inhibited tumor growth by approximately 60 % in in vivo tests, as confirmed by histology [32]. The 5-FU-loaded NPs showed reduced toxicity to renal and liver tissues compared to the free drug. This is significant considering the high incidence and mortality rates of colon cancer. Despite advancements in treatment, there is still a need for better therapeutic options, particularly for recurrent or advanced cases. Utilizing 5-FU-loaded PCL NPs showed 40 times greater anticancer activity in a controlled environment compared to free 5-FU, suggesting their potential as a therapy for advanced colon cancer [33]. The researchers examined the role of E gene expression in colon cancer cell growth and resistance to apoptosis. They found that the expression of this gene significantly enhanced the inhibition of cell growth. The induction of apoptosis through the mitochondrial pathway was effectively achieved by the use of 5-FU-loaded NPs. Cutrim et al. designed a nanofabricated drug delivery system by self-assembling 5-FU onto nitrogen-doped CQDs, enabling controlled release [34]. Nie et al. engineered nanospheres (Fe3O4/Pt-FLU@PEG) containing Pt nanozymes and 5-FU-loaded Fe3O4 NPs for breast cancer treatment. These nanospheres displayed enhanced PMA activity, pH-responsive drug release, elevated ROS and oxygen levels, and significant toxicity against 4T1 cells. Their potential for dual ROS/chemotherapy anticancer therapy was highlighted by notable tumor size reduction and the ability to overcome tumor hypoxia observed in in vivo experiments [35].

This article examines the advantages and disadvantages of various pharmaceutical nanocarriers. Recent scientific studies have revealed their remarkable ability to target a variety of cancer types. The article also explores the examination of the particle size of pharmaceutical nanocarriers using scanning electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering methodologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The significance of 5-FU in tumor therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 depicts the drug 5-FU (5-fluoro-2,4-pyrimidinedione), which effectively inhibits the growth of cancer cells. This drug is commonly used in treating different types of cancer, such as colorectal, breast, and gastric cancer [36].

While 5-FU effectively inhibits the growth and division of cancer cells, it is important to recognize its significant limitations. These include potential complications such as the suppression of blood cells, toxic side effects, inflammation of the mouth, damage to the heart, and harm to the nervous system that can occur with repeated use [30–32]. Additionally, the creation of a specialized and long-lasting nanocarrier platform for 5-FU could help overcome drug resistance and mitigate the potential toxicities linked to the drug [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The benefits of chitosan in nanosystems
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan (CS) is a linear polysaccharide that occurs naturally and has a positive charge. It is obtained from various sources including fungi, crustaceans, and insects [34]. CS is a safe and non-toxic polymer that is widely available and abundant, produced by deacetylating chitin in an alkaline environment and confirmed as such by the US FDA [35].

Chitosan is a biopolymer that is safe, non-toxic, and possesses various beneficial properties such as biocompatibility and antibacterial effects. It is currently being investigated for potential applications in drug delivery, tissue engineering, gene delivery, and wound dressings. Chitosan-based nanocarriers (CSNPs) provide advantages in these areas [37].

CS shows promising potential in cancer therapy by targeting tumor cells and inhibiting their growth through various mechanisms. It exhibits anti-angiogenic, immunomodulatory, antioxidant, and apoptotic effects, while also affecting NF-kB-mediated signaling pathways to slow down cancer cell advancement. Fig. 3 showcases the numerous benefits linked with chitosan nanocarriers [38].

Furthermore, chitosan allows for the pH-responsive delivery of medications within the slightly acidic pH range (5.5–5.8) often present in the cancer microenvironment [39]. Utilizing chitosan as a carrier allows for controlled and sustained release of medications from the formulation, thereby enhancing the efficacy of targeted chemotherapy treatments [40–42].

Chitosan’s positive charge allows it to adhere to biological surfaces and engage in pH-responsive interactions. It can be cross-linked with different substances and is soluble in organic acidic solutions below a pH of 6.5 [43].

The properties of chitosan make it a suitable carrier for hydrophilic drugs such as 5-FU and leucovorin. This has resulted in its increased use in nanomedicine and bioengineering for targeted drug delivery systems, providing enhanced precision, sensitivity, and bioavailability while reducing toxicity [44].
The article analyzes different nano-formulations using chitosan for delivering 5-FU in cancer treatments and emphasizes their potential applications (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chitosan-based nanocarrier improves breast tumor treatment with 5-FU encapsulation
ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles designed for targeted or non-targeted drug delivery typically have small diameters ranging from 1 to 100 nm and exhibit a large surface-to-volume ratio. These characteristics enhance their effectiveness in incorporating, absorbing, and transporting therapeutic agents [45]. In the treatment of breast cancer, nanocarriers are generally classified into two main types: organic and inorganic (Fig. 4 ). Inorganic nanocarriers include quantum dots (QDs), mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN), layered double hydroxide (LDH) nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, and magnetic nanoparticles [46]. Inorganic nanocarriers are known for their superior anticancer drug loading capacity, larger surface area, reduced side effects, enhanced bioavailability, good drug tolerance, and resistance to organic solvents [47]. Conversely, organic nanocarriers consist of polymer nanoparticles, liposomes, micelles, protein nanoparticles, and dendrimers. Organic nanocarriers are preferred for their ease of synthesis and modification, which can improve drug properties, biodistribution, and therapeutic effectiveness. Additionally, organic nanoparticles facilitate sustained drug release and allow for the use of organic solvents [48].

Nanocarriers commonly employed in the treatment of breast cancer include liposomes, dendrimers, micelles, carbon nanotubes, polymer nanoparticles, solid lipid nanoparticles (SLN), and nanostructured lipid carriers (NLC) [49]. Liposomes are utilized for various purposes to enhance the effectiveness of drugs in cases where their levels are insufficient. However, the use of lipids can result in toxicity, and phagocytes quickly break down nanocarriers. Dendrimers have received praise for their high capacity to carry drugs and their bioavailability [50]. Nevertheless, dendrimers face challenges such as rapid elimination, organic composition, and varied properties. Micelles can help in reducing drug toxicity and other adverse effects but are limited to a small number of drugs with low concentrations [51]. Carbon nanotubes can penetrate and target cellular levels, although their material can pose risks. Polymer nanoparticles are biocompatible, degradable, and non-toxic; however, they are less effective and are prone to degradation of the carrier. While SLN has a lower capacity for drug transport and complex structures, it provides advantages in solubility and more controlled drug release [52]. NLC offers numerous benefits over other nanocarriers but is hindered by challenges such as gelation and changes in lipid dispersions. In general, nanocarriers utilized for breast cancer treatment present both advantages and disadvantages. To enhance therapeutic outcomes and address deficiencies, the use of various stimuli can prompt responses from the nanocarriers [53].

Melanoma, known for its high metastatic nature, is one of the most deadly forms of cancer that is resistant to traditional treatments [45]. Treatment for melanoma is costly and not easily accessible to many patients, leading to a high mortality rate. In response, Sahu et al. have developed a cost-effective method utilizing a pH-responsive chitosan-based nano-gel to encapsulate the drug 5-FU. This innovative approach has shown promising results in enhancing the effectiveness and accessibility of melanoma treatment [46].

The drug-loaded nanogel had particle sizes ranging from 100 to 180 nm, which allowed for penetration into tumor blood vessels through the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) phenomenon. The nanogel containing CSNPs had a favorable zeta potential of 43.15 mV due to the presence of NH3 groups [47].

CSNPs enhance the stability and absorption of cancer cells through their positive charge. They facilitate the controlled release of medication, especially in acidic environments, improving targeted drug delivery [48,49].

The hemolysis investigation, conducted in accordance with ISO/TR 7406 ethical guidelines, showed that the nano gel formulation was safe for clinical use, with a hemolytic ratio of less than 4 %. Furthermore, the developed formulation exhibited higher cytotoxicity at lower concentrations (0.2 % w/v) in the HaCaT cell line compared to the commercially available formulation (5 % w/v), suggesting its potential for improved anticancer activity [50].
Tigli Aydin and Pulat developed a pH-responsive nanogel with 70 % entrapment efficiency, demonstrating potential for effective and safe drug delivery in melanoma treatment [51].

The pH-responsive nanocarrier enables efficient drug release in acidic environments, targeting malignant cells while minimizing release in healthy tissues [52]. In vitro release tests showed controlled and sustained release of 5-FU, with higher release in acidic pH similar to tumor conditions, ensuring targeted release at tumor sites without affecting healthy tissues or cells [53]. The pH-responsive 5-FU-CSNPs demonstrate controlled release kinetics following the Higuchi model, with a diffusion mechanism consistent with Fickian diffusion [54].

The NPs were coated with Eudragit L-100 to protect the drug from stomach degradation and enhance release in the distal ileum and colon. Characterization of the NPs included XRD, DSC, SEM, entrapment efficiency, and particle size analysis [55]. NPs formulated with a 1:3 drug to polymer ratio showed excellent entrapment effectiveness, with a drug encapsulation efficiency of 69 [56]. In an in-vitro release trial using the dialysis bag technique, NPs with the enteric coating exhibited the highest cumulative drug release at colonic pH. This suggests that incorporating an enteric coating into the NPs could be a promising approach for targeted drug delivery in the colonic region. Sun et al. developed enclosed CSNPs containing 5-FU with enhanced cellular absorption and excellent stability [57–59].

The nanoparticles loaded with drugs showed similar cytotoxic effects to the 5-FU injection on cancer cells, as proven through in vitro testing on the SGC-7901 gastric tumor cell line using the MTT assay [60]. When 5-FU was incorporated into the nanoparticles, it led to increased mean residence time (MRT) and area under the curve (AUC) in rats compared to the 5-FU solution [61–63].

David and his colleagues developed ionic gelation to create nanocarriers containing 5-FU and quercetin, with the goal of enhancing drug internalization in aggressive pancreatic cancer cells. The study assessed the characteristics and drug release behavior of the NPs [63]. The most optimal release of both drugs occurred at a pH of 5, which was attributed to the maximum swelling of the chitosan material, resulting in faster drug release [64–66]. Chitosan-coated NPs demonstrate potential as an efficient delivery system for chemotherapy drugs in the treatment of pancreatic cancer due to their improved cell uptake [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced cancer treatment utilizing tailored chitosan nanoparticles loaded with 5-FU
ce:section.ce:para: Unctionalized polymer lipid nanoparticles have demonstrated significant potential in cancer therapy, thanks to their small size, compatibility with biological systems, minimal adverse effects, targeted delivery, and high effectiveness in treatment [68–70].

The researchers employed an ionic gelation method to create folate-targeted NPs by combining chitosan (CS), lipids, and imprinting with 5-FU, a chemotherapy drug [71]. The CSNPs demonstrate pH-dependent release in the tumor microenvironment, and folic acid targeting enhances their selectivity. The stability of the chitosan-based NPs was verified by their positive zeta potential [72].

Folate-conjugated nanoparticles showed increased cytotoxicity and decreased cell viability compared to the free drug, especially in HCT 116 and HT-29 cells. The inclusion of folic acid notably enhanced the cellular uptake of the nanoparticles, particularly in the HT-29 cells, indicating their potential for treating colon carcinoma [73–77].

Surface modification of nanocarriers has proven to be a successful strategy for delivering antineoplastic drugs, improving stability, and enhancing therapeutic effectiveness [78]. Chitosan (CS) is considered an excellent candidate for surface modification because of its positive charge, biocompatibility, bioadhesive properties, absorption-enhancing capabilities, and biodegradability [79].

Chitosomes were created by encapsulating 5-FU within liposomes coated with CS in order to enhance its anti-cancer activity in the treatment of colorectal cancer. The CS-coated liposomal systems demonstrated prolonged release of 5-FU, but also led to mild to moderate hemolysis as a result of their positive surface charge [80–82].

Developed N,O-carboxymethyl CSNPs loaded with 5-FU with the goal of targeting cancer cells and reducing drug-related side effects [83]. The formulated nanoparticles showed an initial rapid release of 40 % of the drug within 6 h, followed by sustained release over 48 h [84–87]. The drug-loaded nanoparticles had an initial rapid release caused by surface adsorption, followed by sustained release due to matrix degradation.

A targeted therapy for colon carcinoma was developed using folate-functionalized CSNPs loaded with 5-FU by Ullah et al [88]. The incorporation of 5-FU in the CSNPs helped overcome its limitations in cancer therapy, and the positively charged CS polymer’s mucous-adhesive properties make it a potential carrier system for the treatment of colon carcinoma [89,90].

Folate conjugation improved drug selectivity in tumor cells, reducing harm to healthy cells [91]. Demonstrating potential for targeted delivery through the EPR effect at 5 nm [91], CSNPs with a positive zeta potential of 26 ± 2 enhanced drug permeability across cancerous cell membranes, improving stability [93]. Synthesized NPs have been shown to be a promising localized treatment for colon cancer, utilizing a polymeric blend of nanofibers for controlled drug delivery [92–95]. The mechanical properties of CS nanofiber were enhanced by combining PVA and chitosan, while the addition of PCL to the blend resulted in regulated drug release achieved through cross-linking with glutaraldehyde [96].

The mixture of chitosan-PVA nanofibers showed significant cytotoxic effects on NIH 3 T3 cells, suggesting it as a promising option for antitumor therapy [96]. A nano drug carrier was developed using the ionic gelation technique, combining 5-FU and hyaluronidase within chitosan-PEG-gelatin NPs for targeted and controlled drug release [97]. The hyaluronidase-loaded polymeric nanocomposite enhances substance penetration in tumor tissue, improving the effectiveness of cancer therapy [98–100]. This targeted and controlled release system, utilizing hyaluronidase and cross-linked polymers, has the potential to enhance cancer therapies. The cross-linked nanocomposite demonstrated higher cytotoxicity than 5-FU alone, making it a promising option for targeted therapy [101–103]. NPs were developed using an emulsification solvent diffusion technique, incorporating N-succinyl-chitosan polymer with 5-FU for controlled release in vitro. These NPs exhibit controlled release through diffusion and particle degradation. pH-responsive nanocarriers using chitosan and agarose have been developed for targeted drug delivery and reduced side effects [104–109].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorouracil-loaded nanomaterials: Advancements in combinatorial therapy and Co-administration of 5-fluorouracil with complementary medications
ce:section.ce:para: Zamora-Mora and colleagues developed a magnetic nanoparticle (core–shell) nanosystem with high loading efficiency, dose-dependent cytotoxicity against cancer cells, and potential as a cancer treatment strategy [110–113].

Anitha and colleagues developed thiolated CSNPs for the release of 5-FU in the treatment of colon cancer. The synthesized nanocarriers were biocompatible and displayed a sustained release of medication, with higher release percentages in acidic conditions. Thiolated CSNPs enable site-specific drug delivery, overcoming limitations of 5-FU and colorectal cancer. [114–116].

The nanocarriers that were developed were tested for their anti-tumor effects in vitro using HT29 and IEC6 cell lines. The combination therapy of the nanocarriers led to a higher rate of cell death, decreased wound closure, and increased apoptosis in the HT29 cell line [117].

Salem et al. developed a novel approach for cancer treatment, which involved combining chemotherapy and photothermal therapy. They created a complex consisting of 5-FU loaded CSNPs bound to gold NPs using a composite polymeric-metal nanocarrier. The nanocarrier showed excellent physical stability and a uniform size distribution [118]. Salem et al. discovered that the release of 5-FU from the fictionalized AuNCs bound to 5-FU-CSNPs was pH-dependent, with higher release at lower pH levels. This can be attributed to the protonation of the NH₂ functional group, facilitating the liberation of 5-FU [119].

Guo et al. conducted research and developed a biocompatible carrier specifically for thermal cancer therapy. The carrier consisted of CSNPs enclosing graphitic carbon nanocages loaded with 5-FU (5-FU-CS-GCNCs) (Fig. 5 ). The addition of a CS coating on the nanocarrier improved biocompatibility and reduced cytotoxicity towards cells [120].

The nanocarrier formulation showed a significant decrease in cell viability and an increase in caspase-3 production, leading to disruption of the cell cycle. Protoporphyrin IX (Pp IX) is a potent substance used in photodynamic therapy, which generates reactive oxygen species and selectively eliminates cancer cells [121]. Pp IX quickly and effectively enters tumor tissue due to lower ferric ion concentration and reduced ferrochelatase activity in cancer cells. The zeta potential of the CSNPs varied depending on the pH level, indicating protonation and deprotonation of the amino group in chitosan [122]. The deprotonated CSNPs helped retain drug molecules, creating a pH-responsive drug delivery system. In in vitro release experiments, it was found that the release of 5-FU from the CSNPs was pH-dependent and unaffected by the presence of cerium oxide NPs in the formulation. A flexible nano-hybrid system was developed with 5-FU embedded in CS-coated iron oxide and graphene quantum dots for drug delivery and imaging [123]. Chitosan-coated silver NPs combined with nisin and 5-FU showed potential for enhanced tumor delivery through the EPR effect [124]. The synthesized nano formulation improved the balance of oxidants and antioxidants, cellular organization, and significantly reduced tumor burden and volume [125]. Co-administration of nisin and 5-FU in the nanocarrier formulation decreased the effective doses of both drugs, indicating improved targeting ability. These findings suggest that these formulations have potential as novel treatment strategies in oncology regimens [126]. There is currently a growing interest in co-delivering a combination of chemotherapeutic drugs, offering benefits such as improved bioavailability, enhanced effectiveness at lower concentrations, and reduced toxicity. Fig. 6 illustrates a targeted drug delivery scheme using NPs, allowing for the co-delivery of 5-FU and other drugs. This approach enables lower drug doses, minimizing side effects and increasing treatment effectiveness [127].

Scientists have developed liposomes modified with KLA to effectively deliver both 5-FU and paclitaxel (PTX). This innovative approach aims to improve treatment outcomes specifically for triple-negative breast cancer. In laboratory experiments using MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell lines, these modified liposomes demonstrated reduced systemic toxicity and enhanced targeting of cancer cells [128].

Ni et al. developed lactobionic acid modified poly(ethylene glycol)-poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid)-poly(ethylene glycol) copolymer nanoparticles loaded with curcumin and 5-FU. They demonstrated improved anti-tumor activity with reduced cytotoxicity towards normal blood cells [129–135].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mini systematic review of recent chitosan nanoformulations containing 5-FU
ce:section.ce:para: In the following long-term studies of chitosan-based nanocarriers carrying the anti-inflammatory drug 5-FU, the key findings and main points of these studies are thoroughly examined in Table 2 .

The main difference between our study and others is that ours was the first to evaluate particle size, surface area, drug loading, and encapsulation efficiency. Particle size and surface charge are crucial factors in researching and developing drug nanocarriers [136]. Furthermore, this study examined and analyzed the discrepancies in size between the FESEM and DLS tests. Fig. 7 illustrates the significant factors influencing the production of pharmaceutical nanocarriers. The following section will provide a detailed analysis of these points [137].

Particle size and zeta potential are important factors for nanocarriers as they play a crucial role in determining the stability, drug loading capacity, and target performance of the system. The size of nanocarriers influences their biodistribution, cellular uptake, and in vivo circulation time. Smaller particles can penetrate tissues more effectively, providing a larger surface area for drug absorption and enhancing drug effectiveness [138]. Additionally, size impacts drug release from nanocarriers and may affect pharmacokinetics and bioavailability. Zeta potential, which measures the surface charge of nanoparticles, is vital for their stability and interaction with biological agents. Nanocarriers with high zeta potential repel each other, preventing aggregation and ensuring colloidal stability. This is particularly crucial to prevent premature drug release and ensure the long-term stability of formulations [139]. Zeta potential also plays a role in cellular uptake and targeting ability, as it facilitates interaction with cell membranes and promotes internalization. The schematic illustration in Fig. 8 shows the presence of chitosan particles affecting the surface charge potential of 5-FU [140].

In conclusion, optimizing the size and zeta potential of nanocarriers is essential for enhancing drug delivery performance, improving efficiency, and increasing safety and effectiveness for medical applications [141].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The loading and encapsulation potential of new nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para: The highest rate at which a drug can be effectively contained within a formulation is known as the maximum encapsulation rate. Factors that influence this rate include the compatibility between the drug and excipients, the solubility of the drug, the formulation method, the drug loading capacity of the carrier, particle size, pH levels, ionic strength, temperature, and processing conditions [142]. Achieving a high encapsulation rate is crucial for maximizing the therapeutic benefits of the drug delivery system [143]. A thorough analysis of these factors in the original manuscript is necessary to understand how to optimize drug encapsulation efficiency effectively. In this study, the loading and encapsulation amounts of new nanocarriers developed between 2022 and 2024 are compiled in Table 3 .

The loading value of 88 generally indicates the amount of 5-FU medicine that has been successfully integrated into the nanocarrier system in formulations such as PAA/PVP/CNT/5-FU [144]. This loading value may indicate the percentage of 5-FU in relation to the total weight of the formulation or other relevant metrics. By encapsulating the medication within the nanocarrier, drugs can be embedded into it and benefit from targeted delivery to specific tissues or cells, controlled release, and improved drug stability [145–150]. Various factors, including the characteristics of the medicine, the composition of the carrier, and the manufacturing methods, can affect the loading capacity of nanocarriers. Achieving a loading value of 88 suggests that 5-FU makes up a significant portion of the drug nanocarrier formulation, demonstrating a high drug loading efficiency for this specific system [150–153]. This information is crucial for assessing the delivery of the medication and determining the appropriate dosage for therapeutic effects. Additionally, Fig. 9 displays the maximum and minimum encapsulation values [154–157].

As is well known, the nanocarriers CS-Fe3O4-RGO-CUR, CS/RGO@CUR, and CS/PVP/γ-Alumina/QUE have the highest level of encapsulation. On the other hand, the nanocarrier CS/Bovine Serum Albumin has the lowest level of encapsulation. In the analysis that follows, it was found that the encapsulation values for CS/RGO@5-FU and Fe2O3-CS-MMT/QC nanocarriers are 94.61 and 94, respectively [158–160].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential values of nanocarriers were examined using dynamic light scattering
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers utilized cyclodextrin in conjunction with alginate and chitosan to deliver 5-fluorouracil to the L929 cell line. The dynamic dispersion test revealed that the particle size of the nanocarrier increased with higher concentrations of chitosan. The surface charge potential of +8.07 suggested a strong interaction between the 5-FU and the nanosystem, indicating its suitability for drug release [161].

According to the study conducted by Chen et al., smaller chitosan particles showed a higher tendency to accumulate at the tumor site, indicating their potential for targeted drug delivery. Additionally, these nanoparticles (283.9 nm) displayed outstanding stability, shown by a zeta potential over 30 mV. This strong stability enhances the possibility of using these nanoparticles for effective drug transportation [162].

Wang et al. developed a chitosan-based nanocarrier containing gold nanoparticles to release doxorubicin and 5-fluorouracil for the treatment of glioblastoma. The nanocarrier had a hydrodynamic radius of 196.2 nm and a zeta potential of 16.26. The addition of sodium tripolyphosphate reduced the zeta potential and stability of the nanocarrier [163–166].

Singh et al. used a chitosan-based nanocarrier containing gold nanoparticles and folic acid to deliver 5-fluorouracil to breast cancer cells. In the absence of folic acid, the nanocarrier had a hydrodynamic radius of 126 nm, which increased to 149.3 nm with the addition of folic acid. The zeta potential values for the nanocarrier with gold nanoparticles and the drug, and with folic acid and the drug were 61.9 and 57.9, respectively. Folic acid reduced the zeta potential by decreasing the protonation of chitosan’s amino groups. The optimal zeta potential for drug nanocarriers falls within the range of −30 to +30 mV [167–170].

Keon and colleagues utilized rice stalks embedded in nanowhisker nanoparticles coated with chitosan and iron oxide to develop a nanocarrier for releasing 5-fluorouracil in the treatment of colon cancer. The nanocarrier had a size of 181.31 nm with a zeta potential of 23 mV [171].
Badar et al. investigated nanocarriers containing chitosan, folic acid, and MIL-88B NPs for cancer treatment. The zeta potential was −30.5 mV, but the size was not reported [172].

Pezzo et al. developed a chitosan and polylactic co-glycolic acid composite nanocarrier for releasing 5-fluorouracil in colon cancer patients. The size ranged from 220 to 330 nm, and the zeta potential ranged from 23 to 44 mV [173,174].

In 2023, researchers conducted a study on chitosan nanocarriers with nanographene oxide and agarose to enhance the release of 5-fluorouracil in breast cancer cells. The nanocarriers had an average particle size of 197 nm and a positive zeta potential of 23.5 mV [175].

Hamid et al. conducted a study on chitosan-based nanocarriers with selenium to enhance the delivery of fluorouracil for the treatment of colon cancer [176]. The nanodrug sizes were measured at 251.3 nm (10 mg) and 358.2 nm (20 mg), with corresponding zeta potential values of 41.02 and 31.06, respectively [177]. The positive zeta potential was attributed to the presence of cationic amines on the chitosan surface, which ensured stability and prevented clumping. In another study, chitosan and TPP were used as a cross-linking agent, resulting in a nanocarrier size of 212.25 nm and a surface charge of 22.1 mV [178].

Azdian and colleagues studied a chitosan nanocarrier with agarose for the simultaneous release of 5-FU and curcumin in tumor cells (specifically breast tumors) [31]. The nanocarrier had a zeta potential value of −1.33 mV and a size of 341 nm. The addition of agarose increased the hydrodynamic radius of the nanocarrier, serving as a coating and shell for the drugs. With a dispersion index value of 1.07, the nanocarrier exhibited a monodispersed dispersion, which is desirable for nanocarriers with uniform size distribution [179].

In another study, a chitosan nanocarrier containing Kirin quantum dot NPs, aptamer, and gondric acid was examined for releasing 5-fluorouracil in breast cancer cells. The nanocarrier had a size of 250.6 nm (The nanosystem had a zeta potential of 37.8 mV) [37].

Arshad Farid and colleagues developed a nanocarrier using chitosan, gelatin, and alginate for enhanced skin treatment. The nanocarrier exhibited a negative zeta potential, resulting from the negative charge of alginate [152].

CSNPs containing alginate have been found to be effective carriers for drug delivery, with a size of 254.71 nm that is suitable for skin drug release and can influence the tumor environment [179]. A carrier nanocomposite, developed using polyvinyl pyrrolidene, polyacrylic acid, and carbon nanotubes, has NPs sized around 280 nm, which is ideal for drug release, with a zeta potential value of −8 mV for stability [180]. Fazel et al. investigated chitosan-based nanocarriers with iron oxide NPs and polyacrylic acid, resulting in a size of 129 nm [181].

Researchers explored innovative approaches by conducting a study to investigate the effectiveness of using chitosan nanocarriers that include quantum dot NPs and aptamers for the targeted release of 5-FU. In a breast tumor cell line, they used chitosan nanocarriers incorporating quantum dot NPs and aptamers to release 5-FU. The results revealed a size of 122.7 nm and a zeta potential value of + 31.2 mV [182].
Table 4 showcases the findings from dynamic light scattering tests, providing the particle size and surface potential values of the nanocarriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examining the particle size of pharmaceutical nanocarriers obtained from the electron microscope test
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers utilized cyclodextrin, alginate, and chitosan to administer 5-fluorouracil in L929 cells. Nanocarrier particles, examined under an electron microscope, had a diameter of 270 nm and displayed a dense, spherical structure. However, dynamic light scattering testing resulted in larger particle sizes, potentially attributed to variations in sample preparation techniques [183].

Chen et al. chose chitosan as the medium for releasing the 5-fluorouracil drug. The nanocarrier had a spherical structure with a smooth surface. However, the electron microscope test did not reveal the size of the nanocarrier particles [184].

Meanwhile, Wang and colleagues conducted a study focused on enhancing brain tumor treatment. They developed a chitosan-based nanocarrier supplemented with gold nanoparticles to release doxorubicin and 5-fluorouracil medications. The results demonstrated that the nanocarrier possessed both spherical and triangular shapes [185].

Another study conducted by Singh et al. utilized a chitosan-based nano carrier that included gold nanoparticles and folic acid to release the 5-FU drug in a breast tumor cell line. The research showed that the size of the nanocarrier particles measured 33 nm. However, the light scattering test indicated a particle size of 149.3 nm. This discrepancy may be due to the nanocarrier swelling in a watery environment during the light scattering test, as opposed to the dry state observed in the electron microscope test. In aqueous settings, nanoparticles may aggregate within the nanocarrier, causing an increase in the size of the nanocarrier particles [186].

Keon and colleagues created a nanocarrier by utilizing rice stalks embedded with cellulose nanoparticles, coated with chitosan and iron oxide. This design allows for the effective release of 5-fluorouracil in colon cancer treatment. The nanocarrier exhibits a spherical structure with a diameter of 37.16 nm [187].

Badar and colleagues developed chitosan-based nanocarriers with folic acid and MIL-88B NPs for improved cancer treatment. They observed increased swelling with drug presence and reduced clumping due to chitosan and folic acid. However, they did not report the size of the nanocarriers using electron microscopy [188].

Martin Pezzo and colleagues investigated a composite of chitosan and polylactic co-glycolic acid for enhanced release of 5-fluorouracil in colon cancer. They discovered that the nanocarrier displayed a spherical structure and had particle sizes ranging from 220 to 330 nm, as determined by the dynamic dispersion test [189].

In 2023, researchers investigated the use of chitosan nanocarriers incorporating nanographene oxide and agarose to enhance the release of 5-FU in breast tumors. They discovered that these nanocarriers had a spherical structure ranging in size from 50 to 200 nm, suggesting their potential for delivering drugs effectively in the tumor environment [190].

Researchers conducted an investigation on the cross-linking of chitosan and tripolyphosphate for the release of fluorouracil. The study revealed a nanocarrier size of 212.25 nm and a surface charge of 22.1 mV [191]. In a separate study, Yazdian et al. examined chitosan nanocarriers with agarose for the simultaneous release of 5-fluorouracil and curcumin. They observed a nanoparticle size of 341 nm and confirmed a core–shell structure through electron microscope testing [192].

A chitosan nanocarrier containing carbon quantum dot NPs, aptamer, and gondric acid demonstrated a spherical structure with particle sizes ranging from 100 to 300 nm for releasing 5-fluorouracil in breast cancer cells [48]. Farid et al. designed chitosan-based nanocarriers with gelatin and alginate, showing a dense and porous network structure that facilitated the release of drugs and nanoparticles for improved skin treatment with 5-fluorouracil [51].

A nanocomposite consisting of polyvinylpyrrolidone, polyacrylic acid, and carbon nanotubes was utilized as a nanocarrier for 5-fluorouracil, with particle sizes ranging from 76 to 120 nm. The carbon nanotubes were evenly distributed within the nanocarrier, preserving its structural integrity [193].

In their study, Fazel et al investigated chitosan-based nanocarriers incorporating iron oxide nanoparticles and polyacrylic acid for the release of fluorouracil. The size of the nanocarrier did not affect the results. The incorporation of iron oxide nanoparticles modified the surface morphology of the hydrogel, resulting in accelerated drug release from the nanocarrier [194,195].

In their study, researchers examined chitosan nanocarriers incorporating quantum dot NPs and aptamers for the release of 5-fluorouracil in a breast tumor. The nanocarrier had a size of 122.7 nm, and the quantum dot NPs were observed as spherical structures within the nanocarrier. Table 5 in the research paper contains the particle size and surface potential values of the nanocarriers, as determined by electron microscope testing [196].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size analysis of pharmaceutical other nanocarriers using an electron microscope and dynamic scattering tests
ce:section.ce:para: In Table 6 , the particle sizes obtained from the osmotic electron microscope are reported. The difference in size between the nanocarrier measurements from FESEM and DLS can be attributed to the fact that DLS measures the hydrodynamic diameter of particles in a suspended state, while electron microscope measurements are taken in a solid state. Recent research suggests that the sizes of nanocarriers obtained from DLS are expected to be larger than those from FESEM [44–50].

Curcumin and quercetin are commonly used in chemotherapy because of their antioxidant properties and low toxicity. The size of NPs plays a crucial role in the development of antitumor nanomedicines [197]. A zeta potential range of −30 to + 30 mV is considered highly suitable for designing nano drugs as it ensures the stability of the nanosystem in the bloodstream. Several studies have indicated that the optimal particle size for targeted release of antitumor nanomedicines is below 300 or 200 nm [90–95]. Table 7 provides the particle size of the newly designed nanocarriers, determined through dynamic light scattering testing.

The zeta potential range of −30 to +30 mV is highly suitable for the design of nanocarriers because it ensures their stability and longevity in the bloodstream. Having a zeta potential exceeding 30 mV is particularly advantageous as it creates electrostatic repulsion, preventing the aggregation of particles [190–192]. Nanocarriers with a particle size below 200 nm enhance their distribution within the system and enable better penetration into cancerous tumor tissues [193–195]. The incorporation of nanoparticles increases the size of the nanocarrier, indicating successful synthesis. Additionally, negatively charged nanocomposites exhibit a longer duration in the bloodstream as they move slower compared to positively charged nanocomposites. Previous studies consistently demonstrate that surfactant 80 consistently generates a negative zeta potential [194,195]. A zeta potential value exceeding 30 mV is considered stable based on previous research, providing sufficient electrostatic repulsion to prevent particle aggregation in the nanocarrier. The optimal particle size for targeted drug release in nanocarriers is generally reported to be less than 300 or 200 nm, facilitating effective drug diffusion among tissues and activating the EPR effect.

The difference in size of nanocarriers observed between electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering tests is due to the fact that the light scattering test measures the hydrodynamic diameter of particles in a suspended state, while the electron microscope test measures particles in a solid state [196–200].

A key objective in drug delivery is to achieve a controlled and sustained release of medications over a prolonged period of time. Chitosan, a polysaccharide derived from chitin, has garnered attention as a biomaterial for drug delivery due to its low toxicity, degradability, and compatibility with the human body. However, the rapid release of drugs from chitosan nanoparticles currently hinders their effectiveness in maintaining long-lasting therapeutic effects. To address this challenge and enhance the gradual release of the anticancer drug 5-FU from chitosan carriers, researchers are exploring various approaches. One potential solution involves incorporating different biopolymers or nanomaterials into the chitosan structure to control the release rate. For example, adding poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) nanoparticles or hydrogels to chitosan carriers can create obstacles to slow down the release of 5-FU. Furthermore, modifying chitosan nanoparticles with responsive polymers or pH-sensitive coatings allows for targeted drug delivery. One strategy under investigation is the development of coatings that release the drug specifically in the acidic environment of tumors. This precise delivery method targets cancer cells while reducing the risk of toxic effects on the body as a whole. Additionally, utilizing chitosan derivatives with improved adhesive properties can increase the time that carriers remain in mucosal tissues, such as the gastrointestinal tract, leading to more efficient delivery of 5-FU for treating gastrointestinal cancer. The progress in using chitosan-based carriers to control the release of 5-FU shows promise in enhancing therapeutic outcomes while minimizing side effects. Ongoing research is focused on optimizing the composition and structure of chitosan carriers to achieve sustained and targeted drug release, with the ultimate goal of improving patient results.

Researchers are developing new drug delivery systems to enhance cancer treatment and reduce the side effects of chemotherapy. To meet these challenges, they have designed a variety of nanocarriers that can encapsulate antibodies and deliver them directly to tumors. One major challenge in cancer treatment is delivering chemotherapy drugs effectively to specific locations in the body while minimizing damage to healthy organs. Nanoparticles made of chondroitin sulfate show significant potential in transporting active ingredients, such as drugs or organic chemicals, to address this issue. The use of these nanoparticles improves drug accessibility, reduces harmful side effects, and increases the specificity and effectiveness of chemotherapy treatments. Extensive research has been conducted on the use of chondroitin sulfate nanoparticles in combination with chemotherapy drugs like 5-FU. Promising results have been observed when incorporating 5-FU into carbon nanocarriers, leading to improved targeted delivery of medication to tumors and inhibition of cancer growth. It has also been discovered that nanocarriers with a zeta potential between −30 and +30 mV are stable and long-lasting in the bloodstream, preventing particles from clumping together. Negatively charged nanocomposites remain in the bloodstream for longer periods, and the use of surfactant 80 consistently results in a negative zeta potential. Additionally, differences in nanocarrier size between electron microscope and dynamic light scattering tests can be attributed to variations in measurement methods.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mohammad Hossein Karami is a Post Doctoral Researcher in Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic). He also completed his postdoctoral and doctoral degrees with excellent grades and was a top student. He has published several papers about Nanocomposites, Nanoparticles in Drug Delivery Systems, Nanobio Technology, Wound Healing, Nanomedicine, Cure Kinetics and Degradation Kinetics. His research publications have been cited more than 200 times. He is currently a research assistant at Amir Kabir University of Technology, the Materials and Energy Research Institute, and the Nanotechnology Center of Azad Islamic University, South Tehran Branch. He aspires to read, write, and invent non-stop for 24 h.

Majid Abdouss holds the esteemed position of a Chemistry Professor and serves as the Eminent Endowed Chair on Nanomedicine at Amirkabir University of Technology’s Nanotechnology Institute. With a visionary approach, Majid Abdouss stands as one of the pioneering researchers in the fields of drug delivery, nanomedicine, catalysts, environmental studies, sustainable development, and green industries. Notably, he has authored numerous books and papers in diverse areas such as organic chemistry, drug delivery, catalysts, solar cells, and mineral chemistry. Recognizing the impact and significance of his work, his research publications have garnered over 7359 citations.
